Using the Opposite Reference to find element usages as Tag Values" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3599ca08-9c66-4bb9-bff1-126c83e14c4c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">33975344</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt-include" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0420c8d1-e63b-4794-890f-58e01d55d45d"><ac:parameter ac:name=""><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model" /></ac:link></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="nopanel">true</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303995812 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00235: (2026x Refresh1) Case 2. Finding Element Usages as Types

- page_id: `303995812`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303995812/2026x+Refresh1+Case+2.+Finding+Element+Usages+as+Types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:26:04.672+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

956364775

956364786

956364776

To create a Derived Property to find blocks used as types of a Part Property

1. Right-click a Block to open its **Specification window**.
2. On the left side menu, select **Traceability** > **Create**. **Expression** dialog opens. If you cannot see the **Traceability** node, make sure the **Expert** or **All**mode is enabled. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Select Metachain Navigation.
4. Click Insert .
5. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Block, and under Property , select _typedElementOfType .
6. Click Insert again.
7. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Part Property , and under Property , select Owner . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2020-12-31_14-12-7.png']
8. Click OK .

956364774

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

## PAGE 00236: (2026x Refresh1) Case 3. Requirements from the Owning Package of a Smart Package

- page_id: `303995842`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303995842/2026x+Refresh1+Case+3.+Requirements+from+the+Owning+Package+of+a+Smart+Package
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:26:09.369+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

956399738

956399751

956399741

To create a Smart Package that collects requirements from its owning package

1. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating smart packages' space='MED'] .
2. Open its Specification window and click three dots [ATTACHMENT filename='edit.png'] next to the Query property to edit it. Query dialog opens.
3. On the left side of the dialog, click Simple Navigation .
4. Select Owner and set Is Applied to true .
5. Click the **Use as...**button and select the**Scope of a new Find**command. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Select Find and set the Type to Requirement . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-28_11-42-4.png']
7. Click OK .

956399737

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

## PAGE 00237: (2026x Refresh1) Case 4. Properties that Satisfy System Requirements

- page_id: `303996410`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996410/2026x+Refresh1+Case+4.+Properties+that+Satisfy+System+Requirements
- version_number: 1

- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

956430184

956430195

956430185

To create a Generic Table showing only the properties that satisfy system requirements

1. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating Generic Table' space=''] .
2. Set the Element Type to Property . Make sure to select the Include Custom Types check box. Click OK .
3. For the Scope , click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon****and s elect **Custom** . Query dialog opens.
4. On the left side of the dialog, click Find .
5. Set the Type to Requirement . Make sure to select the Include Subtypes check box. Click OK .
6. Select Metachain Navigation > Context and drag-and-drop the previously created Find on the Context parameter. [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-28_11-47-18.png']
7. Select Metachain Navigation .
8. Click Insert .
9. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Requirement, and under Property , select Supplier Dependency .
10. Click Insert again.
11. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Satisfy , and under Property , select Client . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-28_11-48-40.png']
12. Click OK .

956430183

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

## PAGE 00238: (2026x Refresh1) Case 5. Validating Elements Not Used in Diagram

- page_id: `303996405`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996405/2026x+Refresh1+Case+5.+Validating+Elements+Not+Used+in+Diagram

- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

956458043

956458056

956458046

To validate elements that are not used in a diagram

1. Create a Package and apply the «ValidationSuite» stereotype.
2. Right-click the Package to open its Specification window .
3. On the left side menu, select Constraints > Create .
4. Click three dots [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the **Applied Stereotype** property and set it to **«validationRule»**. Click **Apply**. If you cannot see the **Traceability** node, make sure the **Expert** or **All**mode is enabled.
5. Click three dots [ATTACHMENT filename='edit.png'] next to the Severity , Error Message , and Abbreviation properties to specify them for the created validation rule.
6. Click three dots [ATTACHMENT filename='edit.png'] next to the Constrained E lement property to specify the metaclasses and stereotypes whose instances are validated if not used in the diagram.
7. Click three dots [ATTACHMENT filename='edit.png'] next to the Specification property. Specification dialog opens.
8. From the**Language**drop-down list, select **StructuredExpression**.
9. On the left side of the dialog, click **Create Operation**>****Operation from Model****>**Not.** If you cannot see the **Not**operation, make sure the **Apply Filter** box is not checked.
10. Select A > Reset .
11. Select **A** > **Operation from Model** > **IsEmpty**.
12. Select **Input** > **Operation from Model** > **UsageInDiagramsWithSymbols**.
13. Select Element > Reset .
14. Select Element > Contextual Variable . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-1-26_9-40-39.png']
15. Click OK .

956458042

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303996434 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00239: (2026x Refresh1) Case 6. Requirements Without Text

- page_id: `303996434`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996434/2026x+Refresh1+Case+6.+Requirements+Without+Text
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:26:52.474+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Groovy

1476016285

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Groovy

1476016306

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Groovy

1476016295

To find Requirements without text

1. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating smart packages' space='MED'] .
2. Open its Specification window and click three dots [ATTACHMENT filename='edit.png'] next to the Query property to edit it. The Query dialog opens.
3. Click **Create operation** > **Filter**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Select Input > Find . [ATTACHMENT filename='2_input_param_set_to_find.png']
5. Select Find and set the Type to Requirement . [ATTACHMENT filename='3_type_requirement.png']
6. Select **Predicate**>******Operation from Model**>****IfThenElse****. If you cannot see the **IfThenElse** operation, make sure the **Apply Filter** box is not checked. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Select **Condition** > **Reset**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Select Condition > **Operation from Model** > ****isEmpty**** . [ATTACHMENT filename='8_set_isEmpty.png']
9. Select Input > **Metachain Navigation** . [ATTACHMENT filename='9_set_metachain_navigation_as_input.png']
10. Click Insert .
11. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Requirement, and under Property , select Text . 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
12. Select Then > Boolean. Set the Value to true . [ATTACHMENT filename='12_set_value_to_true.png']
13. Select Else > **Script** . [ATTACHMENT filename='13_set_script_for_else.png']
14. Select the Contextual Variable arg1 > Reset .
15. Select the Contextual Variable arg1 > Metachain Navigation . [ATTACHMENT filename='14_set_variable_to_metachain_nav.png']
16. Set Parameter Name to "text".
17. Click Insert .
18. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Requirement , and under Property , select Text . 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
19. From the Language drop-down list, select Groovy .
20. Insert the following script as the **B****ody**: groovyGroovy0 ? text.iterator().next().trim().length() == 0 : false ; ]]> [IMAGE alt='' src='']
21. Click **OK**. You can view the requirements without text nested under the **Smart Package** in the Containment tree. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303996418 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00240: (2026x Refresh1) Case 7. Sum of Default Values of Recursively Collected Properties Redefined by Specific Property

- page_id: `303996418`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996418/2026x+Refresh1+Case+7.+Sum+of+Default+Values+of+Recursively+Collected+Properties+Redefined+by+Specific+Property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:26:50.881+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1694275867

1694275888

1694275877

Prerequisites:

To get the sum of Default Values of Recursively Collected Properties Redefined by Specific Property

1. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Generic table' space=''] .
2. Set the Element Type to Block and Property (e.g. Value Property, Part Property, etc.) to be displayed.
3. Set the Scope to the Block element which contains the Properties.
4. Click the Columns button and select New Custom Column . Expression dialog opens.
5. Set the Type as Real .
6. Select **Create operation** > **Operation from Model** > [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.1. Sum of Default Values of Property' space='']. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Select Property > Operation from Model > [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.2. Filter Properties Redefined by Specific Property' space=''] . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-29_10-29-15.png']
8. Select Redefines and click three dots [ATTACHMENT filename='edit.png'] next to the Element field to choose a Property that redefines the Properties that need to be collected. [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-29_10-30-16.png']
9. Select Input > Operation from Model > [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition' space=''] . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-29_10-46-4.png']
10. Select **Element** > **Reset**.
11. Select **Element** > **Contextual Variable**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
12. Click OK .

1694275865

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

## PAGE 00241: (2026x Refresh1) Case 7.1. Sum of Default Values of Property

- page_id: `303997851`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997851/2026x+Refresh1+Case+7.1.+Sum+of+Default+Values+of+Property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:28:38.974+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model > (2026x Refresh1) Case 7. Sum of Default Values of Recursively Collected Properties Redefined by Specific Property
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Javascript Rhino

1694243026

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Javascript Rhino

1694243047

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Javascript Rhino

1694243036

To get the sum of the Default Values of a Property

1. Create an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opaque Behavior' space='MED'] named 'Sum of Default Values of Property'.
2. Right-click the Opaque Behavior element/symbol and open its Specification window . Do one of the following:
  1. Click the plus sign next to the **Owned Parameter** property to edit it. If you cannot see the****Owned Parameter****property, make sure the **Expert** mode is enabled.
  2. On the left side menu, select Parameters > Create .
3. In the Specification of Parameter window , set Name to property , Type to Property , Direction to in , and Multiplicity to [0..*] . Click Back .
4. In the Opaque Behavior Specification window , click three dots [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the **Body and Language** property to edit it.
5. In the **Body and Language** dialog, select **Language**>******StructuredExpression**.
6. Select Metachain Navigation .
7. Click Insert .
8. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Property , and under Property , select Default Value .
9. Click **Insert** again.
10. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select LiteralReal , and under Property , select Value . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-29_8-40-12.png']
11. Select Metachain Navigation > Context and set Value to property .
12. Select **Create Operation** > **Operation from Model** > ****Reduce****.******** If you cannot see the **Reduce**operation, make sure the **Apply Filter** box is not checked.
13. Drag and drop the created Metachain Navigation onto Input . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
14. Select Reduce Operation > Script .
15. From the Language drop-down list, select Javascript Rhino .
16. Select Create parameter > Parameter .
17. Insert the following script as the**B****ody**for the**Script**: Javascript Rhino [IMAGE alt='' src='']
18. Click **OK**.

1694243024

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

## PAGE 00242: (2026x Refresh1) Case 7.2. Filter Properties Redefined by Specific Property

- page_id: `303997881`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303997881/2026x+Refresh1+Case+7.2.+Filter+Properties+Redefined+by+Specific+Property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:28:40.984+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model > (2026x Refresh1) Case 7. Sum of Default Values of Recursively Collected Properties Redefined by Specific Property
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1694254134

1694254145

1694254135

To filter Properties Redefined by a Specific Property

1. Create an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opaque Behavior' space='MED'] named 'Filter Properties Redefined by Specific Property'.
2. Right-click the Opaque Behavior element/symbol and open its Specification window . Do one of the following:
  1. Click the plus sign next to the **Owned Parameter** property to edit it. If you cannot see the****Owned Parameter****property, make sure the **Expert** mode is enabled.
  2. On the left side menu, select Parameters > Create .
3. In the Specification of Parameter window , set Name to input , Type to Element , Direction to in , and Multiplicity to [1..*] . Click Back .
4. Create a second parameter.
5. Set Name to redefines , Type to Property , Direction to in , and Multiplicity to [1] . Click Close .
6. In the Opaque Behavior Specification window , click three dots [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the **Body and Language** property to edit it.
7. In the Body and Language dialog, select Language > StructuredExpression .
8. Click **Create Operation** > **Filter**.
9. Select Input > Contextual Variable and set the Value to input . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-28_14-47-40.png']
10. Select Predicate > Operation from Model > Contains . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-28_14-50-1.png']
11. Select **Body** > **Input** > **Metachain Navigation.**
12. Click**Insert**.
13. Under **Metaclass or Stereotype**, select **Property**, and under **Property**, select **Redefined Property**. [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-28_14-49-26.png']
14. Select **Obj** > **Contextual Value** > **redefines**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
15. Click OK .

1694254133

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

## PAGE 00243: (2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition

- page_id: `304000497`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000497/2026x+Refresh1+Case+7.3.+Recursive+Structure+Decomposition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:32:19.734+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model > (2026x Refresh1) Case 7. Sum of Default Values of Recursively Collected Properties Redefined by Specific Property
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1694265108

1694265119

1694265118

To create Recursive Structure Decomposition

1. Create an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opaque Behavior' space='MED'] named 'Recursive Structure Decomposition'.
2. Right-click the Opaque Behavior element/symbol and open its Specification window . Do one of the following:
  1. Click the plus sign next to the **Owned Parameter** property to edit it. If you cannot see the****Owned Parameter****property, make sure the **Expert** mode is enabled.
  2. On the left side menu, select Parameters > Create .
3. In the Specification of Parameter window , set Name to element , Type to Element , Direction to in , and Multiplicity to [1] . Click Back .
4. In the Opaque Behavior Specification window , click three dots [IMAGE alt='' src='']next to the **Body and Language** property to edit it.
5. In the Body and Language dialog, select Language > StructuredExpression .
6. Select Create operation > Implied Relation .
7. Select the Contextual Variable Element and set the Value to element . [ATTACHMENT filename='1_contextual_var_to_element.png']
8. Reset Criteria , Source , and Target parameter values. [ATTACHMENT filename='2_reset_criteria_source_target.png']
9. Select Criteria > Nested Operation . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. Select Criteria > Body > Contextual Variable . [ATTACHMENT filename='4_criteria_body_contextual_var.png']
11. Select Source > Operation from Model > Structure Decomposition . [ATTACHMENT filename='5_source_structure_decomposition.png']
12. Select Source > Body > Element > Reset . [ATTACHMENT filename='6_source_body_element.png']
13. Select Source > Body > Element > Contextual Variable . [ATTACHMENT filename='7_source_body_element_contextual_var.png']
14. Click OK .

1694265107

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

Do one of the following:<ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Click the plus sign next to the <strong>Owned Parameter</strong> property to edit it.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d606259a-c93c-4307-9400-729780fd32d2"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you cannot see the<strong> <strong>Owned Parameter </strong></strong>property, make sure the <strong>Expert</strong> mode is enabled.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>On the left side menu, select <strong>Parameters</strong> &gt; <strong>Create</strong>.</li></ol></li><li>In the <strong>Specification of Parameter window</strong>, set <strong>Name</strong> to <strong>element</strong>, <strong>Type</strong> to <strong>Element</strong>, <strong>Direction</strong> to <strong>in</strong>, and<strong> Multiplicity </strong>to<strong> [1]</strong>. Click <strong>Back</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Opaque Behavior Specification window</strong><span>,</span><strong> </strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">click three dots <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="edit.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>next to the <strong>Body and Language</strong> </span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">property to edit it.</span></li><li>In the <strong>Body and Language</strong> dialog, select <strong>Language </strong>&gt;<strong> </strong><strong>StructuredExpression</strong>.</li><li>Select <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Create operation</strong> &gt; <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Implied Relation</strong>.</li><li>Select the Contextual Variable<strong> Element </strong>and set the<strong> Value</strong> to <strong>element</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="1_contextual_var_to_element.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Reset <strong>Criteria</strong>, <strong>Source</strong>, and <strong>Target</strong> parameter values.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="2_reset_criteria_source_target.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Select <strong>Criteria</strong> &gt; <strong>Nested Operation</strong>.<strong><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3_criteria_nested_operation.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></strong></li><li>Select <strong>Criteria</strong> &gt; <strong>Body </strong>&gt;<strong> </strong><strong>Contextual Variable</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="4_criteria_body_contextual_var.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Select <strong>Source</strong> &gt; <strong>Operation from Model</strong> &gt; <strong>Structure Decomposition</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="5_source_structure_decomposition.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><strong><br /></strong></li><li>Select <strong>Source</strong> &gt; <strong>Body</strong> &gt; <strong>Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Reset</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="6_source_body_element.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Select <strong>Source</strong> &gt; <strong>Body</strong> &gt; <strong>Element</strong> &gt; <strong>Contextual Variable</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7_source_body_element_contextual_var.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.3. Recursive Structure Decomposition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81247c1d-dcd4-4f4c-93a5-4d5a3813d936"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1694265107</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt-include" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="afe4c595-e3f2-4245-8175-5c15b038087e"><ac:parameter ac:name=""><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model" /></ac:link></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="nopanel">true</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303996471 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00244: (2026x Refresh1) Case 8. Showing Parameter Direction and Type in Single Table Cell using StringConcat

- page_id: `303996471`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996471/2026x+Refresh1+Case+8.+Showing+Parameter+Direction+and+Type+in+Single+Table+Cell+using+StringConcat
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:26:58.205+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Javascript Rhino

1724974424

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Javascript Rhino

1724974444

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Javascript Rhino

1724974434

To display Parameter Direction and Parameter Type in a single table cell

1. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Generic table' space=''] .
2. Set the Element Type to Parameter and Activity .
3. Set the Scope to the Activity element which contains the Parameters.
4. Click the Columns button and select New Custom Column . Expression dialog opens.
5. Set the Type as String .
6. Select the Single Value check box. [ATTACHMENT filename='1_type_string_single_value.png']
7. Select **Create operation** > **Operation from Model** > **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.1. Sum of Default Values of Property' space='']fThenElse**. If you cannot see the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case 7.1. Sum of Default Values of Property' space='']fThenElse**operation, make sure the **Apply Filter** box is not checked. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Select Condition > Reset . [ATTACHMENT filename='3_condition_reset.png']
9. Select Condition > Type Test . [ATTACHMENT filename='4_condition_type_test.png']
10. Select Condition and set the Type to Parameter . [ATTACHMENT filename='5_condition_type_parameter.png']
11. Select Then > Operation from Model > StringConcat . [ATTACHMENT filename='6_then_operation_from_model_stringconcat.png']
12. Select A > Reset .
13. Select A > Script . [ATTACHMENT filename='7_a_script.png']
14. Select A > arg1 > Remove . [ATTACHMENT filename='8_a_arg1_remove.png']
15. Select A > Create parameter > Metachain Navigation . [ATTACHMENT filename='9_a_create_parameter_metachain_nav.png']
16. Set the Parameter Name to direction .
17. Click Insert .
18. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Parameter, and under Property , select Direction . [ATTACHMENT filename='10_direction_parameter_direction.png']
19. Select A > Script . From the Language drop-down list, select Javascript Rhino .
20. Insert the following script as the **B****ody**: jsJavascript Rhino0 ? direction.iterator().next().name + " : " : " : " ;]]> [IMAGE alt='' src='']
21. Select B > Reset .
22. Select B > Metachain Navigation . [ATTACHMENT filename='12_b_metachain_nav.png']
23. Click Insert .
24. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Parameter, and under Property , select Type .
25. Click Insert again.
26. Under Metaclass or Stereotype , select Type, and under Property , select Name . [ATTACHMENT filename='13_b_paramter_type_type_name.png']
27. Click OK .

1724974422

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

## PAGE 00245: (2026x Refresh1) Case 9. Requirements Derivation and Satisfaction using Table Hierarchy

- page_id: `303996461`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996461/2026x+Refresh1+Case+9.+Requirements+Derivation+and+Satisfaction+using+Table+Hierarchy
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:26:55.561+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Model analysis > (2026x Refresh1) Specifying criteria for querying model > (2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1660006747

1660006768

1660006758

To perform Requirements Derivation and Satisfaction using the Table Hierarchy

1. Create a **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating Generic Table' space='']**.
2. Set the Element Type to Call Behavior Action , Property , Requirement , State .
3. Set the Scope to the Requirements that will be displayed in the Table.
4. Open the Specification window of the Generic Table and click three dots [IMAGE alt='' src='']next to the Query property to edit it.
5. In the **Query** dialog, select **Custom**.
6. Select Simple Navigation and set:
  - DeriveReqt : Is Applied: **True** Direction: Target to Source
  - Satisfy : Is Applied: **True** Direction: Target to Source
  - Owned Element : Is Applied: **True** Direction: Source to Target
7. Select the Build Hierarchy check box to recursively execute the specified expression for building a multilevel hierarchy starting from the scope as the root. [ATTACHMENT filename='image2021-12-29_14-2-29.png']
8. Click OK .

1660006746

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for Querying the Model' space='']true

The most frequent scenarios for querying the model can be found here. Each case contains detailed instructions for common scenarios found when querying the model.

954700458

INLINE

**Sample model**

The model used in these examples is the *Case Studies for Querying the Model*sample model. To open this model, you need to download *[ATTACHMENT filename='case studies for querying the model.mdzip']*.

##### CaseStudy1Case Study 1 - Modeling user interface for the Report Wizard dialog

This case study provides step-by-step instructions for modeling the **Report Wizard** dialog. If you are already familiar with this case study, please review , or

**Step #1. Create a new Project**

1. Choose New Project from the File menu.
2. Name it Report Wizard .

**Step #2. Create a new Diagram**

1. Click Create Diagram and select User Interface Modeling Diagram .
2. Name the new diagram Report Wizard .

**Step #3. Create Container components**

1. Click the Frame button under the Containers toolbar and drag and drop it on the diagram pane. A Frame component is created.
2. Name the Frame component by doing one of the following:
  - Click the Frame shape on the diagram pane and type Report Wizard.
  - Open the Frame's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Specification window' space=''] .
  1. 
    - Double-click the Frame shape on the diagram pane.
    - In the opened specification window, type Report Wizard in the Title field.
3. Clear the check box properties Maximize and Minimize in the Frame's Specification window.
4. Define an Icon for the Frame symbol:
  1. In the Frame's Specification window, select the Custom option in the Icon property. The Open dialog box opens.
  2. Select an image from your file system and click Open .
5. Under the Containers toolbar, drag and drop two Group Box elements onto the Frame symbol. Under the Other toolbar, drag and drop one Separator element onto the Frame symbol.
  1. Place one Group Box into the other Group Box. Double-click on the larger Group Box and ensure Titled is set to true . In the Title field type Select Template.
  2. Open the specification window of the smaller Group Box and clear the check box property Titled .
  3. Arrange everything appropriately.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Report Wizard Frame with two Group Boxes and one Separator

**Step #4. Create a Tree**

1. Create a new Tree component:
  1. Under the Other toolbar, drag and drop the Tree element onto the Select Template Group Box.
2. Delete old nodes from the Tree component.
3. Create new nodes in the Tree component.
  1. In the Containment tree, right-click on the Tree to invoke its shortcut menu and choose Create Element > Node .
  2. Create five new Nodes in this way and name them as shown below.
4. Add Leaves to Nodes.
  1. You can add a Leaf by right-clicking on a Node in the containment tree and then choosing Create Element > Leaf .
  2. Add at least one Leaf to every Node to indicate that the nodes have internal elements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Report Wizard Window with Added Tree

**Step #5. Add Buttons**

1. Create predefined text buttons.
  1. Under the Buttons toolbar, expand the OK Button group.
  2. Right-click the Back button to expand the text buttons group. Create Back , Next, and Cancel buttons. Guide yourself according to the figure below.
2. Create regular buttons.
  1. Under the Buttons toolbar, drag and drop the Button symbol onto the diagram pane inside the Frame, next to the Select Template Group Box.
  2. Select the created button in the diagram pane and type in New.
  3. Create the remaining buttons by repeating steps 2.a and 2.b. Refer to the figure below.
3. Double-click on the other buttons to invoke their specification window, excluding the Next > , Cancel , New, and Import buttons. Clear the check box Inactive to true.
4. It should look like the figure shown below.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Sample of the Report Wizard window

**Step #6. Using the Report Wizard window**

This User Interface model can now be exported as an image. The steps to do this are as follows:

1. Select Save As Image from the File menu.
2. In the Save As Image dialog, select Active Diagram .
3. In Image File define the location where the image should be placed.
4. Select Joint Photographic Experts Group (.jpg) (or any format you would like) in Image Format and then click Save.

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Notation Formality

982345345897

INLINE

##### CaseStudy2Case Study 2 - Slider Example

This case study contains step-by-step instructions for creating a User Interface model with Sliders. It also shows how to customize the symbol properties of User Interface components. However, it does **not** explain how to create a new project and a new diagram. Please refer to "" for those instructions. If you are already familiar with these case studies, please review

**Step #1 Create a Frame, Labels and Sliders**

1. Create a new Frame .
  1. Under the Containers toolbar, click the Frame button.
  2. Drop it on the diagram pane.
  3. Click on your newly created frame and title Symbol Properties Customization.
2. Add a new Label to the Frame.
  1. Under the Tools toolbar, click the Label button. Place the label onto the frame.
  2. Click the label and change the name to Fill Color.
  3. Create the remaining two labels and name them Text Color and Font Name .
3. Add Sliders to the Frame.
  1. Under the Other toolbar, click the Slider button. Place the slider onto the frame.
  2. Double-click on the Slider to invoke its specification window.
  3. In the opened window, set the Spacing property value to 50.
  4. Set the Knob Position property value to 0 in order to move the knob to the left position.
  5. In the Slider's specification window, select the Values property and click the plus sign button. A Values dialog window opens. If there are values already defined in the window, click the minus sign button to delete them.
  6. To add new values, click the plus sign button and then type 0 Red . 0 Red must be written in a specific way. First, type in 0 and press Enter . Type Red on the next line. Rename the other two given values to 50 Blue and 100 White using the same procedure.
  7. Draw two more sliders and repeat steps 3a - 3f until your frame looks like the one shown in the figure below.

#### NOTE: Notation Formality

Notation Formality

When setting the values of a slider it is important to note that there is no empty space between *0*. For example, *0* represents a new line. When entering a value, it should look like this:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Symbol Properties Customization Frame with Added Labels and Sliders

**Step #2 Add Text Fields**

1. Create Text Fields.
  1. Under the Text toolbar. click the Text Field button. Drop it onto the frame.
  2. Name the text field Red Background .
  3. Repeat the previous two steps for the remaining two text fields, as shown in the figure below.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Added Text Fields to the Frame

**Step #3 Edit Symbol Properties for the Text Fields**

1. Edit Symbol Properties for the first text field.
  1. Select the first text field on the Frame shape.
  2. Right-click it and select Symbol Properties from its shortcut menu.
  3. Set the Use Fill Color property to true and change the Fill Color property value to red. Change the fill color by clicking the ... to the right of the field, and in the Color dialog, click RGB and type in FF0000 in the Color Code field.
2. Edit Symbol Properties for the remaining fields.
  1. Follow steps 1a - 1b for the next steps.
  2. For the second text field, select yellow in Text Color .
  3. For the third one, select the font name Tahoma in Font , as shown in the figure below

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Finalized Slider Example

458973459054

INLINE

##### CaseStudy3Case Study 3 - User Interface Prototyping Example

This case study demonstrates how to connect several user interface models and create a browsable report. The models should represent a test application with a Login Dialog, a Test Browser and a test with several questions. Here are step-by-step instructions on how to add hyperlinks and create browsable reports. If you are already familiar with this case study, please review, or

**Step #1 Create the first Model**

1. Create a new Package for the model.
2. Create a model similar to the one shown below.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']First Model of Prototyping Example - Login Dialog

**Step #2 Create the second Model**

1. Create another new Package for this model.
2. Create a model similar to the one depicted below.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Second Model of Prototyping Example - Test Browser

**Step #3 Create remaining Models**

1. Create a separate Package for each model.
2. You can build models similar to the ones shown in the detailed samples mentioned in the beginning of this chapter, or create your own in a similar manner.

**Step #4 Add Hyperlinks**

1. Add a hyperlink.
  1. Open the model with the Login Dialog.
  2. Open the Specification window of the **OK** button.
  3. Next to the Active Hyperlink property field, click the ... button. The Edit Hyperlink dialog opens.
  4. In the Edit Hyperlink dialog, select Element/Symbol and click the "..." button. Browse to the Package Test Browser , select the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) User Interface Modeling diagram' space=''] in it and confirm two times with OK - a diagram symbol will appear next to the OK button. Double-clicking the OK button will lead to the other diagram.
2. Now connect all the buttons in the other diagrams with hyperlinks. The diagram symbol appears next to a component with a hyperlink.

**Step #5 Create a browsable Report**

1. Create a new report.
  1. Select **Report Wizard** from the **Tools** menu.
  2. Expand the **Default Template** in the tree and select **Web Publisher 2.0**.
  3. Confirm three times by clicking **Next** until the dialog allows you to add data to the report. Add all Packages that contain the created diagrams and click **Next**.
  4. Give the **Report file** a name to the output file. Clear the check box **Display in viewer after generating report**.
  5. Click **Generate**. The report is built and shown in your default browser.

Refer to the figure below.</li></ol></li><li>Double-click on the other buttons to invoke their specification window, excluding the <em>Next &gt;</em><span>,</span><em> Cancel</em><span>,</span><em> New,</em><span> and </span><em>Import </em>buttons. Using the Report Wizard window</strong></p><p>This User Interface model can now be exported as an image. The steps to do this are as follows:</p><ol><li>Select <strong>Save As Image</strong> from the <strong>File</strong> menu.</li><li>In the Save As Image dialog, select <strong>Active Diagram</strong>.</li><li>In <strong>Image File</strong> define the location where the image should be placed.</li><li>Select <em>Joint Photographic Experts Group (.jpg)</em> (or any format you would like) in <strong>Image Format</strong> and then click Save.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="90fee36a-0a2e-4da5-9773-55a3c3df239c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">982345345897</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb43bef2-6b43-4a53-97ba-d5789e0dafe6"><ac:parameter ac:name="">CaseStudy2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Case Study 2 - Slider Example</h3><p>This case study contains step-by-step instructions for creating a User Interface model with Sliders. It also shows how to customize the symbol properties of User Interface components. However, it does <strong>not</strong> explain how to create a new project and a new diagram. Please refer to &quot;<ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy1"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 1 - Modeling user interface for the Report Wizard dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>&quot; for those instructions. <span> If you are already familiar with these case studies, please review</span><span> </span><ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy3"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 3 - User Interface Prototyping Example]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><p><strong>Step #1 Create a Frame, Labels and Sliders</strong></p><ol><li>Create a new <strong>Frame</strong>.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Containers</strong> toolbar, click the <strong>Frame</strong> button.</li><li>Drop it on the diagram pane.</li><li>Click on your newly created frame and title <em>Symbol Properties Customization.</em></li></ol></li><li>Add a new <strong>Label</strong> to the Frame.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Tools</strong> toolbar, click the <strong>Label</strong> button. Place the label onto the frame.</li><li>Click the label and change the name to <em>Fill Color.</em></li><li>Create the remaining two labels and name them <em>Text Color</em> and <em>Font Name</em>.</li></ol></li><li>Add <strong>Sliders</strong> to the Frame.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Other</strong> toolbar, click the <strong>Slider</strong> button. Place the slider onto the frame.</li><li>Double-click on the Slider to invoke its specification window.</li><li>In the opened window, set the <strong>Spacing</strong> property value to 50.</li><li>Set the <strong>Knob Position</strong> property value to 0 in order to move the knob to the left position.</li><li>In the Slider's specification window, select the <strong>Values</strong> property and click the plus sign button. A Values dialog window opens. If there are values already defined in the window, click the minus sign button to delete them. </li><li>To add new values, click the plus sign button and then type <em>0 Red</em>. <em>0 Red</em> must be written in a specific way. First, type in <em>0</em> and press <strong>Enter</strong>. Type <em>Red </em>on the next line. Rename the other two given values to <em>50 Blue</em> and <em>100 White</em> using the same procedure.</li><li>Draw two more sliders and repeat steps 3a - 3f until your frame looks like the one shown in the figure below.</li></ol></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8625b9f4-ffb5-4447-9e18-e673dc79fc66"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Notation Formality</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When setting the values of a slider it is important to note that there is no empty space between <em>0</em>. For example, <em>0</em> represents a new line. When entering a value, it should look like this:</p><p><ac:image ac:align="left" ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2017-07-22 at 1.03.50 PM.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="256" ac:width="501"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav53d3c9355ce1fa8f8aeb943c85e060d5.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> Symbol Properties Customization Frame with Added Labels and Sliders</h6><p><strong>Step #2 Add Text Fields</strong></p><ol><li>Create Text Fields.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Text</strong> toolbar. click the <strong>Text Field</strong> button. Drop it onto the frame. </li><li>Name the text field <em>Red Background</em>.</li><li>Repeat the previous two steps for the remaining two text fields, as shown in the figure below.</li></ol></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="255" ac:width="501"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0212b54c35d95fb5cb12d97d73b2c6a3.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Added Text Fields to the Frame</h6><p><strong>Step #3 Edit Symbol Properties for the Text Fields</strong></p><ol><li>Edit <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> for the first text field.<ol><li>Select the first text field on the Frame shape.</li><li>Right-click it and select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> from its shortcut menu.</li><li>Set the <strong>Use Fill Color</strong> property to true and change the <strong>Fill Color</strong> property value to red. Change the fill color by clicking the ... to the right of the field, and in the Color dialog, click <strong>RGB </strong>and type in <em>FF0000</em> in the <strong>Color Code</strong> field.</li></ol></li><li>Edit <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> for the remaining fields.<ol><li>Follow steps 1a - 1b for the next steps.</li><li>For the second text field, select yellow in <strong>Text Color</strong>.</li><li>For the third one, select the font name Tahoma in <strong>Font</strong>, as shown in the figure below </li></ol></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="257" ac:width="500"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav4a6ff3bdd67c8337921149a2d11507f0.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Finalized Slider Example</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="83028fde-0aa2-472a-aac9-b0d6acc6c427"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">458973459054</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="223a7361-ed93-4ecd-b934-a2513a86262c"><ac:parameter ac:name="">CaseStudy3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Case Study 3 - User Interface Prototyping Example</h3><p>This case study demonstrates how to connect several user interface models and create a browsable report. The models should represent a test application with a Login Dialog, a Test Browser and a test with several questions. Here are step-by-step instructions on how to add hyperlinks and create browsable reports. <span>If you are already familiar with this case study, please review </span><ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy2"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 2 - Slider Example]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span>, or <ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy1"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 1 - Modeling user interface for the Report Wizard dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p><p><strong>Step #1 Create the first Model</strong></p><ol><li><p><span>Create a new Package for the model.</span></p></li><li><p><span>Create a model similar to the one shown below.</span></p></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="179" ac:width="318"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0c437b6b5c5cf1161007d0bd842f87e7.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">First Model of Prototyping Example - Login Dialog</span></h6><p><strong>Step #2 Create the second Model</strong></p><ol><li><p>Create another new Package for this model.</p></li><li><p>Create a model similar to the one depicted below.</p></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="238" ac:width="488"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavc6cb8ac9da4f9b8f2fe40099cf5f9e86.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Second Model of Prototyping Example - Test Browser</h6><p><strong>Step #3 Create remaining Models</strong></p><ol><li><p>Create a separate Package for each model.</p></li><li><p>You can build models similar to the ones shown in the detailed samples mentioned in the beginning of this chapter, or create your own in a similar manner.</p></li></ol><p><strong>Step #4 Add Hyperlinks</strong></p><ol><li><p>Add a hyperlink.</p><ol><li><p>Open the model with the Login Dialog.</p></li><li><p>Open the Specification window of the <strong>OK</strong> button.</p></li><li>Next to the Active Hyperlink property field, click the ... button. The <strong>Edit Hyperlink</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>In the Edit Hyperlink dialog, select <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Element/Symbol</strong> and click the &quot;...&quot; button. Browse to the Package <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Test Browser</strong>, select the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) User Interface Modeling diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[User Interface diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in it and confirm two times with OK - a diagram symbol will appear next to the OK button. Double-clicking the OK button will lead to the other diagram.</li></ol></li><li><p>Now connect all the buttons in the other diagrams with hyperlinks. The diagram symbol appears next to a component with a hyperlink.</p></li></ol><p><strong>Step #5 Create a browsable Report</strong></p><ol><li><p>Create a new report.</p><ol><li><p>Select <strong>Report Wizard</strong> from the <strong>Tools</strong> menu.</p></li><li><p>Expand the <strong>Default Template</strong> in the tree and select <strong>Web Publisher 2.0</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Confirm three times by clicking <strong>Next</strong> until the dialog allows you to add data to the report. Add all Packages that contain the created diagrams and click <strong>Next</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Give the <strong>Report file</strong> a name to the output file. Clear the check box <strong>Display in viewer after generating report</strong>. </p></li><li><p>Click <strong>Generate</strong>. The report is built and shown in your default browser.</p></li></ol></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6420b013-e83a-4c61-852d-115bb9202c8f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">433458345987534</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related References:</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) UML diagrams" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Extension diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Extension diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7450a3ef-4b10-43be-b787-2410909608bb"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) User Interface Modeling diagram" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304000631 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00248: (2026x Refresh1) Case Study

- page_id: `304000631`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000631/2026x+Refresh1+Case+Study
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:32:29.653+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Procedures > (2026x Refresh1) Displaying information indirectly related to element through Derived Properties
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Let’s say, we need to show Warning Order and Medical Condition exchange elements that are produced and consumed by the performer Search Node. Exchange elements are information indirectly related to the Search Node element.

We will analyze the OV-2 diagram from the DoDAF sample project.

First, we will show the exchange elements produced by the Search Node.

Let’s create the **Produces Exchange Element** derived property for the performer *Search Node*. Open the *Search Node* **Specification** window and in the **Traceability** property group click the **Create** button. In the opened **Criterion Editor** dialog, select the **Meta Chain** tab. Then specify the derived property name and the expression for the meta chain as it is shown in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specification of derived property name and expression

In the following figure, you can see that a new subgroup named **Other** is created in the performer **Specification**window. Our created **Produces Exchange Element** derived property is placed under this subgroup. The **Produces Exchange Element** derived property has *Warning Order* and *Medical Condition* exchange elements as its values.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of newly created derived property

Next, we need to display values of the **Produces Exchange Element** derived property for the *Search Node* performer in the OV-2 diagram.

A derived property is applicable to an element type, not the specific element. In this particular example, our created derived properties are applicable to all performers, not only to*Search Node*.

We can use a Note element wherein we can display information indirectly related to the *Search Node*. Let’s draw a Note and connect it to the Search Node performer. Then click the Edit Compartment as it is shown in the following figure. In the **Compartment Edit** dialog, select the **Element Properties** tab. From the properties list, select to display the **Produces Exchange Element** property. After clicking the **OK** button, *Warning Order* and *Medical Condition* exchange elements are displayed in the Note.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Information indirectly related to Search Node is displayed in Note

Now, let’s create a generic table wherein we can also display information indirectly related to the *Search Node*, as well as to other performers. For that purpose, we select Performer as an **Element Type** and add existing performers to the table. Then we select which information to display as new columns. Previously created **Produces Exchange Element** derived property is also available to add as a new column through the **Show Columns** menu (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Produces Exchange Element derived property displayed as column

Now, let’s create a new derived property directly in the generic table. This property will show which exchange elements are consumed by the performer element. We can do that using the same **Criterion Editor** and doing the same actions as described previously. In the following figure is the example of the **Consumes Exchange Element** derived property expression.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Consumes Exchange Element derived property expression

When the expression is specified, the newly created derived property is displayed as a new column (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Consumes Exchange Element derived property added as new column

#### PANEL: Related procedure

Related procedure

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Displaying information indirectly related to element through Derived Properties' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Open the <em>Search Node</em> <strong>Specification</strong> window and in the <strong>Traceability</strong> property group click the <strong>Create</strong> button. In the opened <strong>Criterion Editor</strong> dialog, select the <strong>Meta Chain</strong> tab. Then specify the derived property name and the expression for the meta chain as it is shown in the following figure.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_I.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Specification of derived property name and expression</h6><p>In the following figure, you can see that a new subgroup named <strong>Other</strong> is created in the performer <strong>Specification </strong>window. The <strong>Produces Exchange Element</strong> derived property has <em>Warning Order</em> and <em>Medical Condition</em> exchange elements as its values.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_II.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of newly created derived property</h6><p>Next, we need to display values of the <strong>Produces Exchange Element</strong> derived property for the <em>Search Node</em> performer in the OV-2 diagram.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="073cc4b6-5bb5-4f7b-85af-bd43a30b1661"><ac:rich-text-body><p>A derived property is applicable to an element type, not the specific element. In this particular example, our created derived properties are applicable to all performers, not only to<em> Search Node</em>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>We can use a Note element wherein we can display information indirectly related to the <em>Search Node</em>. Let’s draw a Note and connect it to the Search Node performer. Then click the Edit Compartment as it is shown in the following figure. In the <strong>Compartment Edit</strong> dialog, select the <strong>Element Properties</strong> tab. From the properties list, select to display the <strong>Produces Exchange Element</strong> property. After clicking the <strong>OK</strong> button, <em>Warning Order</em> and <em>Medical Condition</em> exchange elements are displayed in the Note.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_III.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Information indirectly related to Search Node is displayed in Note</h6><p>Now, let’s create a generic table wherein we can also display information indirectly related to the <em>Search Node</em>, as well as to other performers. For that purpose, we select Performer as an <strong>Element Type</strong> and add existing performers to the table. Then we select which information to display as new columns. Previously created <strong>Produces Exchange Element</strong> derived property is also available to add as a new column through the <strong>Show Columns</strong> menu (see the following figure).</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_IV.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Produces Exchange Element derived property displayed as column</h6><p>Now, let’s create a new derived property directly in the generic table. This property will show which exchange elements are consumed by the performer element. We can do that using the same <strong>Criterion Editor</strong> and doing the same actions as described previously. In the following figure is the example of the <strong>Consumes Exchange Element</strong> derived property expression.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_V.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Consumes Exchange Element derived property expression</h6><p>When the expression is specified, the newly created derived property is displayed as a new column (see the following figure).</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_VI.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Consumes Exchange Element derived property added as new column</h6></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="56056506-16fd-4b38-a100-47b4499cf79e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedure</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Displaying information indirectly related to element through Derived Properties" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303991169 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00250: (2026x Refresh1) Change impact analysis

- page_id: `303991169`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303991169/2026x+Refresh1+Change+impact+analysis
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:20:25.570+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Requirements management > (2026x Refresh1) Analyzing Requirements
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Impact analysis minimizes the risk of introducing undesirable effects to the system by increasing your understanding of how the proposed change might affect the elements in the system change. Once a change is proposed its impact should be identified.

Traceability links and visualization of related elements display dependencies of requirements and help you see the full impact of a proposed change in those requirements.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Impact analysis minimizes the risk of introducing undesirable effects to the system by increasing your understanding of how the proposed change might affect the elements in the system change. Once a change is proposed its impact should be identified.</p><p>Traceability links and visualization of related elements display dependencies of requirements and help you see the full impact of a proposed change in those requirements.</p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303999258 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00251: (2026x Refresh1) Changed Property Types

- page_id: `303999258`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303999258/2026x+Refresh1+Changed+Property+Types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:30:42.944+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Migration User Guides > (2026x Refresh1) UPDM projects migration manual > (2026x Refresh1) UPDM 2.1 migration to UAF 1.1 > (2026x Refresh1) Mapping issues and solutions
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### Changes for Condition and Environment

In earlier versions, the Activity Performable Under Condition property (owned by Activity) had the Condition as its value. After the migration, the newly created Actual Condition with the Condition set as a type, replaces this property.

The same is valid for the Required Environment (owned by Location Holder) value. If the value was Environment, the Actual Environment is created with Environment set to this property. If the value was Condition, it is removed and added to ToDo property.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Changes for Doctrine

In earlier versions the Doctrine property (owned by Capability Configuration) had Constraint for its value. In UAF 1.1 Constraint is replaced by Standard Operational Activity. After the migration, Capability Configuration is set as constrainedElement to Constraint Except if Constraint is Operational Constraint or Service Policy.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h4>Changes for Condition and Environment</h4><p>In earlier versions, the Activity Performable Under Condition property (owned by Activity) had the Condition as its value. After the migration, the newly created Actual Condition with the Condition set as a type, replaces this property. </p><p>The same is valid for the Required Environment (owned by Location Holder) value. I<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">f the value was Environment, the Actual Environment is created with Environment set to this property. If the value was Condition, it is removed and added to ToDo property.</span></p><p><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="concition_to_instance.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Changed Property Types" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h4>Changes for Doctrine</h4><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In earlier versions the Doctrine property (owned by Capability Configuration) had Constraint for its value. In UAF 1.1 Constraint is replaced by Standard Operational Activity. After the migration, Capability Configuration is set as constrainedElement to Constraint Except if Constraint is Operational Constraint or Service Policy.</span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303999047 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00252: (2026x Refresh1) Changed Property Types in UAF 1.2

- page_id: `303999047`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303999047/2026x+Refresh1+Changed+Property+Types+in+UAF+1.2
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:30:25.323+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Migration User Guides > (2026x Refresh1) UPDM projects migration manual > (2026x Refresh1) UPDM 2.1 migration to UAF 1.2 > (2026x Refresh1) UPDM 2.1 to UAF 1.2. Mapping issues and solutions
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### Changes for Condition and Environment

In earlier versions, the Activity Performable Under Condition property (owned by Activity) had the Condition as its value. After the migration, the newly created Actual Condition with the Condition set as a type, replaces this property.

The same is valid for the Required Environment (owned by Location Holder) value. If the value was Environment, the Actual Environment is created with Environment set to this property. If the value was Condition, it is removed and added to ToDo property.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Changes for Doctrine

In earlier versions the Doctrine property (owned by Capability Configuration) had Constraint for its value. In UAF 1.3 Constraint is replaced by Standard Operational Activity. After the migration, Capability Configuration is set as constrainedElement to Constraint Except if Constraint is Operational Constraint or Service Policy.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h4>Changes for Condition and Environment</h4><p>In earlier versions, the Activity Performable Under Condition property (owned by Activity) had the Condition as its value. After the migration, the newly created Actual Condition with the Condition set as a type, replaces this property. </p><p>The same is valid for the Required Environment (owned by Location Holder) value. I<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">f the value was Environment, the Actual Environment is created with Environment set to this property. If the value was Condition, it is removed and added to ToDo property.</span></p><p><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="concition_to_instance.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Changed Property Types in UAF 1.3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h4>Changes for Doctrine</h4><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In earlier versions the Doctrine property (owned by Capability Configuration) had Constraint for its value. In UAF 1.3 Constraint is replaced by Standard Operational Activity. After the migration, Capability Configuration is set as constrainedElement to Constraint Except if Constraint is Operational Constraint or Service Policy.</span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303999023 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00253: (2026x Refresh1) Changes for Security Control

- page_id: `303999023`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303999023/2026x+Refresh1+Changes+for+Security+Control
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:30:23.263+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Migration User Guides > (2026x Refresh1) UAF projects migration manual > (2026x Refresh1) Migration from UPDM 3 (UAF 1.0 beta) to UAF 1.1 > (2026x Refresh1) Migration from UPDM 3 (UAF 1.0 beta) to UAF 1.0
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The new approach for modeling the Security Control (and its specialization) is based on [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Requirement' space='MED']. The profile changes are described in the following table:

| Before migration | After migration | Comment |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Name | Type | Name | Type |  |
| Security Control | Activity | Security Control | Class | Aditionally, Security Process is created to overtake the information, that cannot be stored in Security Control [Class]. |
| Security Control Family | Comment | Security Control Family | Class | The Criterion from SecurityControlFamily [comment] is moved to the Text of the SecurityControlFamily [class, requirement].Information about the Annotated Element is moved to ToDo. |
| Enhanced Security Control | Activity | Enhanced Security Control | Class | Aditionally, Security Process is created to overtake the information, that cannot be stored in Security Control [Class]. |
| Security Control Action | Call Behavior Action | Security Process Action | Call Behavior Action |  |
| - | - | Security Process | Activity | Security Process is a new element dedicated for modeling security behaviors. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
